headline: Bridge: A pass, followed by accurate use of slam convention, leads to the optimal Grand
summary: When pre-empting, be especially aggressive when opponents have yet to describe their hands
body_text:
For Christmas, a maximum contract. Would your partnership have reached it?

BiddingDealer: NorthLove All

East’s intervention was good, but not good enough. If he overcalls 4H — as happened at some tables — reaching 6C proved the limit. If you feel that your opponents definitely have game, and possibly a slam, and they have not revealed their shape, be super-aggressive.

As usual, bidding any grand slam would have scored very highly, but 7NT was achieved at only two tables.

Here, South’s initial pass was correct; a double would normally show a stopper in the opponent’s suit. North, who might — without the intervention — have been considering rebidding no-trumps, now showed his suit. South used Roman Key-card Blackwood and, on hearing that partner held three key-cards, bid the next suit up to ask for Q♣. North’s 6D indicated that he held the trump queen, plus K♦. Now, South’s 6H bid was merely a noise to see whether North could bid 6S. When he did, South bid 7NT, knowing that if North
